subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Reaction rates depend on concentration, temperature, and catalysts; rate laws are determined experimentally, not from the balanced equation.
core_concepts: The rate law $\text{rate} = k[A]^m[B]^n$ must be determined experimentally from initial-rate data; exponents are NOT stoichiometric coefficients.
Integrated rate laws allow finding concentration at any time: zero order ($[A]_t = [A]_0 - kt$), first order ($\ln[A]_t = -kt + \ln[A]_0$), second order ($1/[A]_t = kt + 1/[A]_0$).
First-order half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ is constant (independent of initial concentration); zero and second order half-lives depend on concentration.
The Arrhenius equation $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$ shows that rate constant increases exponentially with temperature; catalysts lower $E_a$ without being consumed.
A reaction mechanism consists of elementary steps; the rate law for an elementary step IS written from its stoichiometry; the slow step is rate-determining.
Collision theory: molecules must collide with correct orientation AND sufficient energy ($\geq E_a$) to react.
An energy diagram shows reactants, products, transition state (peak), $E_a$, and $\Delta H$; catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower $E_a$.

key_terms.definition: $\text{rate} = k[A]^m[B]^n$; determined by experiment; orders $m$ and $n$ are not stoichiometric coefficients
Proportionality constant in rate law; increases with temperature; units depend on overall order
Minimum energy required for a successful collision; catalysts lower $E_a$
Substance that increases rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower $E_a$; not consumed
Time for concentration to decrease by 50%; constant for first-order reactions
Sequence of elementary steps summing to the overall balanced equation
Slowest elementary step; its rate law equals the overall rate law
Highest-energy, unstable arrangement of atoms at the peak of the energy diagram

exam_tip: To identify reaction order from data, look at which graph is linear: $[A]$ vs $t$ (zero order), $\ln[A]$ vs $t$ (first order), or $1/[A]$ vs $t$ (second order).
